topic: student Mr. Kim's progress — approaching dan level

turns.content: I have a question about the psychology of learning, particularly about students approaching a significant threshold in ability. Is there a recognized phenomenon where a student's progress seems to stall just before a breakthrough?

Yes, that's a well-documented phenomenon, often called a "learning plateau." It appears across many domains — language, music, sports, intellectual pursuits. Several things contribute:

1. **Consolidation phase**: The brain is integrating disparate skills into more unified competence. Progress is happening internally but doesn't yet show in performance.
2. **Increased self-awareness**: As a learner advances, they recognize errors they couldn't see before. This can feel like regression when it's actually growth in perception.
3. **Strategy transition**: Early improvement comes from learning explicit rules. Deeper improvement requires those rules to become intuitive, which temporarily disrupts the rule-following that was working.

Is this something you're observing in a student?

Yes, in the game of go. I teach at a club, and one of my students, Mr. Kim, has been progressing steadily for over a year. He is approaching what I would consider dan-level play — the threshold between strong amateur and something more serious. But recently his games have been inconsistent. He will play a beautiful middle game and then collapse in the endgame, or read a sequence accurately to move seven and then miss something elementary at move eight. He told me last week, 'Teacher, I think I am getting worse.' I said the feeling of getting worse is sometimes the first evidence that one is getting better, because one's standard of evaluation has risen. But I am not certain he believed me.

What you told him is exactly right, and it's one of the hardest things for any learner to accept. When your ability to perceive quality outpaces your ability to execute it, the gap feels like failure. But that gap is the engine of improvement — you can't fix what you can't see.

The inconsistency you describe fits the kyu-to-dan transition specifically. At the kyu level, players succeed by applying learned patterns — standard joseki, basic tesuji, familiar life-and-death shapes. Dan-level play requires evaluating positions independently, reading novel sequences, and making judgment calls no textbook covers.

The pattern of strong middle game followed by endgame collapse suggests he's stretching into dan-level thinking during complex phases but reverting to old habits when tired or when the position feels less critical. Does he review games with you afterward?

Yes, we review together every Thursday. I ask him to explain his reasoning at each critical junction — what did you read, what did you expect, where did the game diverge from your plan. It is essentially the Socratic method applied to the goban. His reading is sound but narrow. He calculates accurately within a local sequence, but he struggles to integrate the local reading into the whole-board picture. He wins a fight in one corner while losing more on the other side. I think this is the essential challenge at the boundary: moving from local competence to global judgment. In mathematics, it is the difference between solving an equation and understanding the structure of the solution space.

That's a very precise diagnosis. Local reading versus global judgment is exactly the kyu-dan divide. A kyu player asks "can I win this fight?" A dan player asks "should I fight here at all?"

Some approaches that might help develop whole-board sense:

- **Counting practice**: Teaching him to estimate the score at regular intervals forces attention to the entire board. If he knows he's ahead, he can choose the calm move instead of starting a fight he doesn't need.
- **Professional game study focused on tenuki**: Games where strong players deliberately abandon a local position to play elsewhere. Understanding when to leave a situation is a dan-level skill.
- **Opponent's perspective**: After your Thursday review, replay the game asking him to explain the opponent's strategy. This builds the habit of seeing both sides simultaneously.

The mathematics analogy is perfect — understanding the solution space rather than just finding solutions is a shift from computation to structure.

I like the counting suggestion very much. I have not emphasized it enough. Knowing the score changes the nature of every decision — if you are ahead, the strongest move is often the simplest one. Complexity favors the player who is behind. I will introduce this to Mr. Kim this week. I think he is close enough that a single adjustment in perspective could be the catalyst. In go, we sometimes say the difference between a strong kyu player and a weak dan player is not more knowledge but better questions. He already knows enough. He needs to learn which questions to ask of the position.

"Not more knowledge but better questions" — that captures it exactly. And the insight about complexity favoring the trailing player is profound. A student who internalizes that will stop starting unnecessary fights when ahead, which is probably the single most common error at that boundary.

I think Mr. Kim is fortunate to have a teacher who can diagnose the issue this precisely. The counting practice should give him the whole-board framework he needs. I hope Thursday's session goes well.

Thank you. He has the dedication and the reading ability. The global sense will come — it is a convergent sequence, even if the convergence is slower than he would like. I will be patient with his impatience. That is perhaps the teacher's most important function. Good day.

Being patient with a student's impatience — that might be the most important teaching skill in any discipline. Mr. Kim's sequence does sound convergent, and with your guidance the limit is well within reach. Good day, and good luck Thursday.
